Threat.value: Suddenly feeling or acting as if the stressful experience were actually happening again
Being superalert or watchful or on guard

Negative Appraisals.value: I feel like an object, not like a person

Maladaptive Strategies.value: do not make any plans for the future
ruminate about how event could have been prevented

Memory.value: I can't seem to put the frightening event into words.
My memories of the frightening event are very clear and detailed.
When I have memories of what happened I sometimes hear things in my head that I heard during the frightening event.

vignette:
He is a 22-year-old divorced man from the Middle East who presents with prominent post-traumatic distress following an accident. His PCL-5 score of 61 is consistent with a severe symptom burden. Since the event, he has become increasingly withdrawn and tense in daily life, describing nights disrupted by trouble falling or staying asleep and days shaped by a persistent sense of being on guard. He is easily unsettled when he feels himself being watched, and uniformed authority figures are particularly activating. Stress in general now leaves him feeling immediately keyed up, and at times he suddenly experiences the accident as if it were happening again.

His recollections remain strikingly vivid and detailed, yet he also reports that he cannot put the frightening experience into words. At times, sounds from the accident seem to return in his mind, making the memory feel invasive and alive rather than past. These trauma memories sit alongside a broader sense of threat that keeps him vigilant and watchful, scanning his surroundings and struggling to settle.

He also describes enduring changes in how he sees himself and other people. He feels “dead inside,” and at times as though he is merely an object rather than a person. His trust in others has been eroded, and he now expects people cannot be trusted. These appraisals appear to shape the narrowness of his life, with little future orientation and no plans being made ahead. He spends considerable time ruminating about how the event might have been prevented.

In response, he increasingly avoids going out. His world has become smaller and more restricted, and this pattern has begun to affect his functioning and relationships. The combination of vivid trauma memories, ongoing hypervigilance, and a persistent sense of threat maintains his distress, leaving him exhausted, socially distant, and stuck in a cycle that has been difficult to break.
